title: Sri Lankan Devilled Chicken

ingredients.name: boneless chicken, cut into bite-sized pieces
visual_checks: Thigh meat stays juicier than breast; cut into 3 cm cubes for even cooking.
ingredients.notes: Thigh meat stays juicier than breast; cut into 3 cm cubes for even cooking.

subs.name: 400 g extra-firm tofu (pressed, cubed, fried crispy) for a vegetarian devilled dish — the sauce is the star and works with tofu beautifully.

ingredients.name: onion, cut into thick rings
visual_checks: Leave slightly thick so they stay crunchy.
ingredients.notes: Leave slightly thick so they stay crunchy.

ingredients.name: capsicum, cut into chunks (use both colours if possible)

subs.name: Coconut aminos or 1 tsp Worcestershire sauce for a gluten-free alternative with a similar savoury depth.

ingredients.name: vinegar
visual_checks: White or malt vinegar; rice vinegar gives a milder tang.
ingredients.notes: White or malt vinegar; rice vinegar gives a milder tang.

subs.name: 1 tbsp fresh lime juice added at the end — brighter, slightly less sharp than vinegar.

steps.text: Season the chicken pieces generously with salt and a pinch of turmeric if desired. Heat 3 tbsp oil in a kadai over medium-high heat. Shallow-fry the chicken pieces in a single layer for 5–6 minutes, turning once, until golden and cooked through. Remove and set aside on a plate lined with paper towels.

In the same kadai (add the remaining 1 tbsp oil if it looks dry), increase heat to high. Add garlic, ginger, dried red chillies, and curry leaves (if using) and stir for 30 seconds — the chillies will darken and the kitchen will fill with a pungent aroma.
Add the onion rings and slit green chillies. Toss on high heat for 2 minutes — the onion should soften but keep its structure and light crunch. Add the capsicum chunks and toss for 1 more minute.
Add the tomato ketchup, soy sauce, and vinegar. Stir quickly to combine with the vegetables — the sauce will bubble and caramelise slightly on the hot pan, smelling sweet and tangy.

Return the fried chicken to the pan. Toss everything together on high heat for 2 minutes until each piece of chicken is well coated in the sticky, glowing sauce. Taste and adjust salt, vinegar, or chilli.
Remove from heat and serve immediately as a starter or alongside fried rice. Devilled chicken is best eaten hot and fresh — the sauce becomes sticky and the chicken stays crisp.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: soy-sauce → Coconut aminos or 1 tsp Worcestershire sauce for a gluten-free alternative with a similar savoury depth.; chicken → 400 g extra-firm tofu (pressed, cubed, fried crispy) for a vegetarian devilled dish — the sauce is the star and works with tofu beautifully.; vinegar → 1 tbsp fresh lime juice added at the end — brighter, slightly less sharp than vinegar.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook entirely in a kadai — no special equipment needed. Soy sauce is widely available in Indian supermarkets and online. The flavour profile is close to Indo-Chinese chilli chicken; use the same wok technique of keeping the flame high and working fast. Serve with veg fried rice or as a party starter.
